name: Katsuki bakugo- beat up
description:
Heyyyy, sooo I’m sad and single (actually I’m not but i want bakugo) sooo I’ve made this! Sorry not sorry, guys! Have fuuuun Ilysm xxx

NOT MY ART

The first thing Bakugo always noticed was the ringing.

It came after the shouting. After the crash of something heavy against the wall. After the sharp, brutal thud that knocked the air from his lungs and left him curled on the floorboards.

The ringing filled his ears like static, drowning out everything else.

Tonight was no different.

His father’s voice had been loud—too loud—spitting words that blurred together into nothing but anger. Bakugo hadn’t even bothered arguing this time. Arguing only made it worse. He’d learned that years ago.

The first hit had caught him across the cheek, snapping his head to the side so hard his vision sparked white. The second had driven into his ribs. By the third, he was on the ground, palms scraping against the floor as he tried to brace himself. He hated how his body reacted—how it curled inward instinctively, how his arms shielded his head without him telling them to.

He hated that he knew the routine.

A kick to his side. Another to his back.

His breath wouldn’t come. His lungs felt crushed, like someone had wrung them out and left them empty. Something in his ribs burned hot and sharp every time he tried to inhale. He tasted iron when he swallowed.

He didn’t cry. He never cried there.

That only made it worse, too.

Eventually, like always, it ended with the same sentence.

“Get out.”

It wasn’t shouted this time. Just flat. Final.

Bakugo staggered to his feet, gripping the wall for balance. His legs felt unsteady, one knee threatening to buckle as soon as he put weight on it. His cheek throbbed in time with his heartbeat, swelling already. He didn’t bother checking the mirror. He didn’t need to.

He grabbed his hoodie from the hook by the door and left without another word.

The night air hit him like a slap. Cold. Damp. It seeped into his skin and settled there. He shoved his hands into his pockets and started walking.

He always walked.

The abandoned bowling alley sat on the edge of town, its neon sign long dead, the letters half-hanging like broken teeth. The windows were boarded up, graffiti splashed across the brick in messy colors. To anyone else, it was just another rotting building.

To him, it was refuge.

He slipped through the loose board at the back entrance, ducking inside with practiced ease. The smell of dust and old carpet greeted him. It was quiet—always quiet—except for the faint hum of the city far away.

Moonlight filtered through cracks in the boards, casting pale stripes across the lanes. The polished wood was dull now, coated in grime. Pins lay scattered near the end of one lane like forgotten bones.

He made his way to the same spot he always used—behind the old counter near the shoe racks. There was a nest of blankets he’d dragged here months ag
